* % gain or loss in this table is as part of our total U.S. portfolio value (including non-core). ECG stock rose 35% in the second quarter, resulting in a 0.7% in total portfolio gains, for example.

Stock Commentary

Top Losers

Progressive

Progressive is the least interesting of our performance detractors this quarter, and the least concerning in my view. It has been our second biggest loser two quarters in a row – reflective of how big a position it is – and is just above flat on the year. Its earnings estimates continue to go up.

That usually is a contradiction – earnings drive stock prices. But the medium-term concerns are reasonable. Progressive’s earnings are so high because it is posting very low combined ratios. That tends to be unsustainable because claims regress to closer to what Progressive priced, or Progressive lowers rates. If Progressive doesn’t lower rates, competitors come in. Indeed, Progressive’s policy in force growth rate in August slowed down to 8.5% on an annualized, month over month basis, and 13% year over year, compared to 18% at the beginning of the year.

At the same time, Progressive trades for less than 13x trailing earnings, is growing policies at an 8.5%-13% pace, and has done fine through cycles. We have an order to fill out the position for portfolios where we are underweight, and not terribly worried about it over the years ahead.

Lululemon

Lululemon is on the loser list for the 3rd quarter in a row. It’s fair to say I got the analysis wrong, even as things I couldn’t quite predict fouled up the picture. The company suffers from tariffs and especially the de minimis application of tariffs on smaller shipments. It is fending off competition from Alo and Vuori and other big clothing retailers trying to muscle in on athleisure. And the part I think I got most wrong is that international growth is not strong enough to pick up the slack.

The shares are now quite cheap. If/when the company gets back to growing earnings, it could be a very attractive proposition. It doesn’t seem like that turning point is imminent, however. I’ve reduced our stake, both for tax loss purposes and given the bleak near-term outlook. We’ll see if it flips, and also see if this becomes contagious for Deckers Outdoor (DECK), another of our holdings.

Portillo’s

We also got Portillo’s wrong, and quickly. The company had a disappointing Q2 report, lowering guidance. But I bought more shares, as the company was sticking to its strategy and its issues – especially getting a foothold with its newer restaurants in Texas - seemed plausible and solvable.

A month later, the company lowered its guidance and changed its strategy, slowing its growth plans. I don’t think that’s a bad move, necessarily, but it shows the company is a bit lost at the moment. Tariffs and inflation are part of the problem; McDonald’s is pressuring other fast-food companies with a value play; and the ICE crackdown on the Hispanic community probably hurts Portillo’s on the margin. But unsteady management is always concerning.

Indeed, a week after we sold half our position for tax losses, Portillo’s announced it was looking for a new CEO. The concept is very profitable, but does it play outside of Peoria and Chicago? I remain intrigued, but we’ll watch with a smaller position for the time being.

New Stocks

Corpay (CPAY)

Corpay, formerly known as Fleetcor, is a payments company. I’ve always struggled with understanding payments companies as there are so many different companies taking a bite of the pie and the value proposition. Corpay’s business is easier to grasp, though. Its fastest growing business is corporate payments, where it helps companies manage their accounts payable and do cross-border transactions. It also has a classic fuel card business, allowing companies to oversee their spending on gas or electric charging; and a lodging business, which targets hotel payments when people need to stay for emergency reasons, flight delays, or other business reasons.

The company juggles around its business lines, buying companies and then divesting units regularly. But it has grown its sales 8.5% per year for the last five years, and its earnings at a 7% clip. It trades at less than 15x 2025 cash earnings. I like how we can hedge against the price of oil – if gas prices go up, Corpay’s revenues go up – without investing in an oil & gas company. This is not the easiest company to understand, but the growth track record and runway going forward and the valuation have earned it a place in our portfolio. I should say this was an idea I first read about on Thomas Lott’s service, Cash Flow Compounders.

GOGO Inc (GOGO)

We’ve owned private jet in-flight connectivity provider Gogo before. I have my questions about the business, its competition with Starlink, and management. But the management team is new, the company is finally close to launching its 5G service and has launched its low earth orbit satellite service, and if we have turned a corner, the stock could do really well.

You can read my full argument here.

Hillenbrand (HI) & Brighthouse Financial (BHF)

You’ll notice in the winners section that we had luck with a couple reported deals that finally came to fruition. Hillenbrand and Brighthouse are similar type bets.

There are reports Hillenbrand is putting itself up for sale and that bids should come in soon. It is an industrial company that makes equipment for companies developing plastics. It is in a cyclical low point, and for now pays a good dividend and is profitable when you exclude one-off issues. My rule with these is to only invest if I’m comfortable owning the stock if a deal does not play out. We opened a small position feeling there is real value, and that a deal process might lead to it being realized soon.

There is also extensive reporting that Brighthouse is in negotiations to be sold. One of the insurance company’s biggest shareholders has come out in favor of the deal. That could be a bad sign about management’s intentions to sell, but the company – which is a spin-off of Metlife and sells life insurance, with a complicated hedging strategy – has been a bit of a mess as a public company. Our position is smaller here, as I feel worse about owning this in a no-deal scenario.

TFS Financial (TFSL)

One more Thomas Lott idea. This is a small Ohio bank that doesn’t seem cheap, but that’s because most of its shareholders are shareholders in the mutual holding company, who waive their rights to the dividend annually. TFS is interesting, then, because it pays out an 8%+ dividend, making it a viable bond replacement, and because it benefits from interest rate cuts. We have just seen interest rates cut for the first time in 2025, and more cuts may be in store. We have stocks, like Charles Schwab, that benefit from higher interest rates, so it’s good to have the other side of the equation. And for now, this is just taking the place of a bond in one of our portfolios.

Air Lease (AL)

Air Lease is a smaller competitor to AerCap (AER), which we’ve owned for a long time. Air Lease agreed to a buyout. We bought shares after the deal was announced, so no big spike. Instead, we bought for two scenarios. Scenario 1 is nothing dramatic happens, we earn 3.2% in tax-advantaged accounts over the next 6-9 months, more or less equivalent to a treasury bill. Scenario 2 is that another buyer comes in and bids more for the company, as the agreed-to takeout price is a steal (a cheaper valuation than AerCap’s, for example). I don’t think scenario 2 is that likely, but it’s enough that I feel it worth buying a small position. Scenario 3 would be the deal falling apart, but Air Lease stock would still be cheap, and there’s no specific reason (anti-trust, financing) that would get in the way of the deal closing.

Fabrica Italiana Lapis ed Affini (FILA.MI)

In our European portfolio, we opened a small position in FILA. This is not a shoe company, but instead a pencil and brushes company. The idea comes from Overlooked Alpha. Essentially, FILA – owner of Dixon Ticonderoga, the pencils – owns a meaningful stake in Doms Industries, an Indian paper and arts company, that leaves the rest of FILA trading too cheaply.

Winners: A Bunch of Buyouts

Wrapping up, let’s talk about our four buyouts.

Dallas News was in our portfolio as a negative enterprise value company trying to turn around its newspaper operations. Newspapers is a tough business, and I’m not sure if the company would have finished the journey. But a sale was always logical, and it agreed to sell itself to the Hearst Corporation. Hearst runs the Houston Chronicle, which struck me as a good paper the one time I read it a couple years ago, and the offer was attractive.

Alden Global then made a larger offer. This ultimately forced Hearst to pay $16.5 instead of $14 for Dallas shares. Alden Global has a (deserved reputation) for gutting newspapers. While its final offer was $20/share, I was happy to take Hearst’s $16.5. At some point, a fiduciary responsibility includes thinking of what the news environment will be in the future for the political body, economy, and investors. We sold 2/3 of our position on the day the news came out – shares that were in tax-advantaged accounts or that would surely not reach a long-term gain – and then sold the rest across September at various parts in the deal process.

We rolled some of those DALN sales into a speculation in WideOpenWest (WOW). This is a cable company. I would not want to have owned it, but there was a standing offer to go private from a majority shareholder over a year prior, at $4.8/share, and some reporting that negotiations were continuing. We bought shares at $3.93 and then $3.74. A few weeks later, the company announced an agreement with that majority shareholder for $5.2/share. The final merger filings suggest really poor negotiating on the part of the management, but that wasn’t our problem.

Vimeo’s (VMEO) merger story also took a long time to play out. We bought on reports about a deal in March 2024. The deal didn’t come through, but I liked Vimeo’s ability to get back to growth and profitability. That was playing out in my view, but it was going to take time. Fortunately for us, Bending Spoons, the rumored buyer a year ago, came back and offered $7.85/share. While we did sell some of our shares too early at $4, we saw a nice boost in several portfolios from this.

The last buyout was the least expected. Steelcase, a major position for us I discussed last quarter, agreed to merge with HNI Corp (HNI). HNI corp previously bought Kimball International in 2023; we sold our shares then and missed out on owning HNI. This time around, merger docs show Steelcase did not shop itself around, and it probably could have squeezed more from a full deal process. But the merger makes sense, and SCS shareholders will own 36% of the combined company. We don’t plan on selling any of our shares in the near future, though we’ll happily take the $7.2/share cash HNI is offering as part of the exchange.

Stocks We Sold

Besides the buyouts mentioned, we close positions in Atkore (ATKR) and LICT (OTCPK:LICT).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Moving From Sell To Hold Amid Yellow Flags (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Not long ago, in June to be precise, I assigned a sell rating to The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD). Unfortunately (I'm not short, so I have no reason to hope the stock falls), the analysis aged like fine wine, with the stock falling about 28%.

My reasoning was quite simple: although it is a fast-growing stock, TTD's valuation was not the best, especially when considering stock-based compensation. In addition, at the time, I argued that although the company has moats, it was not impenetrable against the likes of Amazon (AMZN) or Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), by the way, two companies in which I hold positions.

The 28% drop in the stock made the valuation more reasonable, and although it is still not a huge bargain, nor does it have fewer risks related to disruption now, it is enough to say that at the current price, TTD is a solid hold. The playbook is simple: sometimes pessimism is so great that it becomes the classic “buy on the sound of cannons, sell on the sound of trumpets,” but in this case, I don't think it's enough to buy yet.

TTD Is Battling With Giants

The core of The Trade Desk's appeal is its independence. It is a company that has no bias (because it does not own any media) and can “buy the entire internet,” very different from a platform like Amazon's or Google's DSP. This will certainly continue to be important for many Trade Desk customers, as it allows them to see the platform as fairer and allows Trade Desk to focus on the right goals, which is to make partnerships with customers work regardless of where the ad is.

However, in my view, this independence is still a double-edged sword, as it is also where the company's vulnerability lies. The company is directly involved in an escalating war with giant conglomerates, and the risk is that there could be a price war.

For example, Mercado Libre (MELI) stock recently fell because one of its main markets is Brazil, and Amazon also wants a share of the market and made an aggressive move to waive all logistics fees in the coming months. The reason is simple: in the long run, this may make sense for Amazon, depending on the LTV of third-party sellers, and since it is an important market and Amazon has virtually infinite resources for certain types of moves, it makes sense.

Returning to the ad industry, similar moves are happening. Google recently announced that it will lower fees to grow DSP and match Amazon's close-to-zero DSP fees, according to Ad Age. Again, for these companies, these moves make sense, as DSP is just one end of the ecosystem.

But for The Trade Desk, DSP is practically its entire thesis. As a pure-play, it cannot compete in this war on price, only with its key differences such as independence, data, and technology, which, if they cease to be valuable to the customer, will be disrupted.

To the credit of management, the company is not standing still. The fight should not be fought on price, but mainly on innovation to bring a better return on ad spend. Kokai is one example, which is the AI-powered upgrade, and in the earnings call, the company mentioned that customers who have adopted Kokai are seeing many improvements and are spending more than 20% faster on the platform compared to those who have not used Kokai.

Audience Unlimited is another new product from the company that leverages AI to score and bundle third-party data to provide better access and a lower cost. Finally, Unified ID 2.0 (or UID2) is the company's open source initiative that is an attempt to respond to the death of third-party cookies, which is also an interesting initiative.

These are moves that do not make the moat so wide, but it is still a relevant moat, and in any case, it is a way for the company to further differentiate itself in this industry.

TTD’s Valuation: No Longer Expensive, But Not Yet A Bargain

Q2 earnings were solid, with revenue growing 19% YoY, but one of the main reasons that triggered market fears was the outlook for Q3, which is 14% YoY growth. This is a clear slowdown, especially when we consider the context of competition in the industry.

However, it turns out that double-digit growth for TTD's revenue in the coming years is not exactly bad, especially after the significant loss in market cap.

The forward P/E is now 28x, and looking out to 2026, this multiple falls to 24x, which is interesting for a company that is still present in a market with very positive prospects and should be able to capture a share of this growth. Note in the chart below that if the company manages to maintain this accelerated pace of EPS growth, compounding occurs, and the multiple falls to single digits in about five years.

But still, it's not the most compelling valuation in the world. The comparison with peers is a little unfair, since Google and Amazon have other businesses, but in my view it is a sufficient comparison, since these are companies that are also growing rapidly and have larger moats than TTD.

In any case, the table below makes it clear that compared to these two and Meta (META), the valuation is not that appealing. The price-to-earnings ratio for 2026 is 24x, but Meta's is the same, and Google's is even lower than that. The price-to-sales ratio is also higher than Google's. Most importantly, stock-based compensation continues to be a factor that hinders this compounding, as you can see in the GAAP P/E ratio, which is 59x.

The Bottom Line

The Trade Desk's industry, despite having strong growth prospects, is also experiencing intense competition, which greatly increases uncertainty for the company. Despite this, there are reasons to be a little more optimistic.

TTD is trying to navigate this scenario through initiatives and differentiation, and has also undergone a valuation reset. For this reason, I believe the company is no longer a “sell.” However, neither the valuation nor the outlook for the coming years is clear enough for a buy rating, so my upgrade is “only” to a hold.

data.title: Rumble Plus Perplexity: Headline Heat, Minimal Meat (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Rumble Inc.'s (NASDAQ:RUM) stock is popping after the video-sharing platform reported it entered into a “strategic partnership” with Perplexity.

When I first heard the headline, I assumed that Rumble was licensing its data to Perplexity to train its AI models, just as Reddit, Inc. (RDDT) has done in the past. However, after reading the press release, it appears that Rumble is predominantly utilizing Perplexity technology to enhance its platform.

Not that there is any issue with Rumble leveraging AI to improve its platform. That is a prudent and natural extension of its operations. But the press release does come off as slightly melodramatic. Moreover, as there was no 8-K, it was not treated as a material definitive agreement, and the PR lacks substance (e.g., no revenue share, terms, KPIs, SLAs, etc.).

I last covered Rumble in March. My rating was “Sell” after concluding that its “current valuation is out of touch with reality.” My discounted cash flow analysis showed that Rumble’s valuation implied expectations for 55% Y/Y revenue growth and 18% FCF margins over the course of nine years. However, the company was growing 39% Y/Y and not generating any cash upon my last review.

RUM has underperformed the index (SPY) since my March write-up. However, because the stock is up 16% in intraday trading following the Perplexity PR, the gap has narrowed.

The article that follows revisits RUM in light of recent developments.

Trends

Before diving in, I like to establish operational/financial trends and valuation assumptions.

Although Rumble is not a pure “SaaS” play, it should enjoy many benefits associated with SaaS businesses, like high margins, recurring revenue (Rumble Premium), network effects, and ARPU expansion levers (bundles, à-la-carte features).

I’d like to point out that although Rumble was formed in 2013, it didn’t really “take off” until the pandemic (2020). With that said, the massive growth that was spurred by the pandemic has waned considerably. In the past four quarters, Y/Y growth was 39%, 48%, 24%, and 12%, respectively (percentages were rounded).

Some things are headed in the right direction. For instance, Rumble’s gross margins are nearing 0% (oddly, this is an improvement). In its second quarter, Rumble generated $25.085 million in revenue (up just 12% Y/Y). Its “cost of services” was $26.542 million. Because expenses like G&A, R&D, and S&M remained relatively steady, Rumble’s losses (from operations) narrowed ($26.639 million in Q2).

Judging from the numbers, Rumble remains "sub-scale" (negative margins, heavy opex, and results sensitive to non-cash mark-to-market items).

A RUM investment ultimately hinges on its path to sustained positive gross margin and opex leverage.

As my discounted cash flow model that follows implies, RUM is still valued as if it will successfully scale.

Valuation Assumptions

Inputs include $108 million in “Year 1 revenue” (the consensus estimate), 20% Y/Y revenue growth (based on recent performance), and 10% FCF margin (assumes business becomes profitable over time). Everything else is either standard DCF methodology or was pulled from RUM financial data.

I believe that my assumptions are optimistic because Rumble has not yet proven it can generate cash, and maintaining 20% Y/Y revenue growth throughout the projection (nine years) will not be easy. Even with optimistic assumptions, RUM is overvalued by nearly 80% per my model.

After some tinkering, I ascertained that RUM is priced for anywhere between “46%-55% Y/Y revenue growth” and “14% to 22% FCF margin.”

In my experience, the fact that RUM’s valuation is so detached from reality is a testament to it being a meme stock. However, as I cannot speculate on speculation (RUM’s short-term valuation is mainly driven by speculation, not fundamentals), I have to keep focused on the fundamentals.

Verticals

Fundamentally, what is most concerning about Rumble, for me, is that the company is actively expanding into other verticals (e.g., AI, cloud, crypto) without first establishing its core offerings (video) as a viable business. Imagine if a child were losing money from his lemonade stand and then asked his dad for money to also sell bikes.

The verticals are all “buzzwords,” and, in my opinion, Rumble Cloud is the only tangible offering, but with unproven market traction. AI is helpful for engagement but trivial to Rumble’s valuation in the near-term. In August, Rumble released a press release stating it intends to acquire "AI company" Northern Data, which, supposedly, “if completed, would fundamentally transform Rumble into a global AI cloud leader.”

Upon a closer look at the proposed deal details, it would result in considerable dilution for RUM shareholders (~169 million new shares per my math). Rumble would be effectively trying to buy scale in AI/HPC overnight. So, while Northern Data could accelerate Rumble Cloud, at face value, the deal would be massively dilutive, customer-concentrated (Tether), and execution-heavy.

During Rumble’s latest earnings call, management appeared to have poured cold water (“offer is preliminary and subject to additional work”) over the deal. In my experience, it is unusual for a company to issue a PR regarding a “planned” acquisition. Subsequently, in my opinion, it appears that Rumble was just “testing the waters” to see if the deal’s news would go over well with the market. Judging by the stock’s reaction, it didn’t.

Financial Health

As of its last report, Rumble had $283.8 million in cash and equivalents. It does not have any significant debt obligations.

In Q2, Rumble’s “cash burn” (cash from operations) was $15.9 million. When I divide its cash by this figure, it implies a “cash runway” extending for a few years.

While Rumble has the financial means to turn its core business around, it has limited flexibility in its pursuit of verticals.

Final Thoughts

Rumble’s most recent press release sees the company, again, headlining buzzwords (“AI,” “cloud,” “crypto”). While the company is pursuing trendy verticals, its core business’s growth is decelerating and remains unprofitable. It goes without saying that RUM’s valuation is, at the very least, questionable. As my DCF model implies, RUM is extraordinarily overvalued (it breaks my model). With that said, RUM is what it is (a meme stock), so its performance, especially in the short run, may be tethered not to fundamentals but to hype. However, from a fundamental standpoint, there is a lot of reason for caution (rating downgrade to “Strong Sell”).

My Strong Sell rating, which I do not use often, results from the combination of 1) poor fundamentals (declining KPIs in its core business, prospect of dilution, ongoing cash burn, and speculative M&A activity) and 2) a rich valuation.

Some things that can change my mind include sustained gross profit and operating leverage (opex/revenue <100%) for over two consecutive quarters, MAU re-acceleration, clear signs that Rumble Cloud is scalable, AI partnership ROI, ad stack proof (take-rate improvement), and disciplined capital deployment (no large dilutive deals without clear ROI).

data.title: CHY: Discounted Valuation On This Steady Convertible Fund (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

With market indexes continuing to break new all time highs, investors may start to get a bit cautious about the possibility of a pullback in the near-term. Additionally, we still see uncertainty in the markets related to things like how tariffs will impact industries, or the U.S. government shutdown. Income funds like Calamos Convertible & High Income Fund (NASDAQ:CHY) can be an efficient way to hedge against these uncertainties. When I previously covered CHY, I issued a Hold rating due to the premium valuation and heavy use of leverage. Since then, there have been some shifts in the macroeconomic environment, so I wanted to revisit this fund to provide updated insights into its performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Additionally, I've realized that CHY's price has now fallen into the discount to NAV range. For instance, CHY now trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 3.18%. For reference, CHY has traded at a premium to NAV of 3.68% over the last five year period. Therefore, the current discount can be an indication that it is a great time to begin accumulating shares. To better conceptualize the current discount in relation to its historical range over the last ten years, I added the red line in the graph below. As we can see, CHY sits at a larger discount relative to its historical pricing range.

CHY has an inception dating back to 2003, which means we have over two decades of operating history to reference. The fund also has a management fee of 0.8%. Additionally, the fund has done a solid job at preserving investor capital over a longer holding period. Investors seeking capital preservation and income reliability may be happy with CHY's long-term track record. Now that markets have seen its first interest rate cut of the year, CHY's appeal may increase over the next twelve months, assuming additional interest rate cuts will follow. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy that CHY implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fact sheet, CHY has total managed assets of $1.33B that is spread across a diverse range of assets. The fund's primary objective is to provide attractive total returns through the combination of capital appreciation and current income. To achieve this, the fund takes a blended approach between convertible securities and several other income focused assets.

As their name implies, convertible securities typically operate as bonds that produce income, but have the ability to be converted into common shares if management believes the position has the ability to appreciate in value. However, common stock only accounts for 0.2% of assets at the moment, so management may be very selective on which securities are actually converted.

Additionally, this blend of different income focused assets is meant to offset the vulnerability to rising interest rates, compared to traditional fixed income funds. To mitigate risks, CHY also spreads its assets across a diverse range of sectors. As we can see below, CHY is most heavily allocated to the Information Technology sector at 27.2% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Consumer Discretionary and Industrials investments, which accounts for 12% and 10.7% of assets respectively.

Since the fund doesn't try to replicate the weighting of any specific index, we get a rather unique mix of holdings. For instance, the largest position is in The Boeing Company (BA) at 2.4% of assets. This is followed by many other notable names such as Uber Technologies (UBER), Strategy (MSTR), CyberArk Software (CYBR), and Live Nation Entertainment (LYV), just to name a few.

Performance Risk

Although a part of the fund's object is capital appreciation, I believe that investors should limit their expectation around its ability to provide a meaningful level of growth. For instance, the performance chart below indicates that the price has only increased by 5.73% over the last decade. While there were certainly instances where CHY's price rapidly increased, such as 2021, these are outliers. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 181% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of CHY's value comes from its ability to continue paying out large distributions over time.

So who is a fund like CHY meant for? Well, I believe that CHY is a good fit for investors that want to prioritize capital preservation through market uncertainty. CHY has done a great job at recovering from market declines and keeping investor capital protected over a longer holding period. While there are other places to get a large yield in the market, many of these alternatives have higher volatility levels. This includes other asset classes like business development companies, closed end funds, or master limited partnerships.

Conversely, CHY may not be the best choice for investors that want to maximize total return potential. Although CHY has exposure to high growth companies, the fund maintains positions through income securities, which will not participate in the full growth of these assets. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that CHY should only be utilized by investors that are specifically seeking income generation and capital preservation above all else.

However, I want to specifically warn investors that a higher yield doesn't automatically result in a higher total return. Although CHY offers a high dividend yield, the fund hasn't demonstrated resilience during periods of market declines. A good example of this can be seen when measuring the performance over 2022's market decline, which was driven by the combination of record breaking inflationary data and rapid interest rate hikes.

As we can see below, CHY suffered a total loss of over 28%, despite offering a large double-digit yield. This was a larger loss than the traditional index-tracking ETF, SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), over the same period. While CHY was ultimately able to recover from this decline, this is still a vulnerability that investors should be aware of.

Since CHY focuses on income securities that may be sensitive to interest rates, the fund has demonstrated a vulnerability to shifts in interest rates. Additionally, CHY utilizes leverage as part of its operating strategy in an effort to amplify earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 34.41% of assets, which is equivalent to $458M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this high interest rate environment and may be a contributor to CHY's suppressed share price. Investors need to consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated.

As we can see below, CHY was capable of rapidly increasing throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels. The lower cost of debt meant that CHY's operating spread between its cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities was larger. This allowed the fund to see its valuation and earnings increase. Conversely, CHY's share price has remained suppressed since interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023 to their decade highs. The Fed has recently initiated a smaller 25 basis point cut to rates, but this may not be enough to drive meaningful growth yet.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.10 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.6%. If we take the current monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.20 per share. Looking at the dividend history below, CHY has been able to maintain consistent payouts for the majority of the last decade. The exception was the period around the pandemic where payouts were slightly adjusted. However, the fund was able to quickly get payouts back to its prior levels.

According to the latest available semi-annual report for 2025, CHY is capable of generating earnings that outpace the payouts. For instance, CHY generated $2.19 in earnings for 2024, which was enough to support the payouts for more than a year and a half. Unfortunately, the payout history can be a bit staggered, based on market conditions. Since the semi-annual report is only dated through the end of April, the market rally that was experienced in the quarter after that is missed in this report. So although the semi-annual report indicates a loss of $0.16 per share for the first half of 2025, I anticipate that the performance will be much stronger by the time that the full annual report for the year is released. The breakdown of annual earnings amounts is as follows:

  • 2020: $2.86 per share
  • 2021: $4.05 per share
  • 2022: ($4.10) per share
  • 2023: ($0.13) per share
  • 2024: $2.19 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): ($0.16) per share.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from CHY may not be the most tax-efficient. For instance, the dividend history below indicates that there are periods where the payouts are fueled by short-term capital gains, which have the highest tax burden for investors. If you want to ensure that CHY's distributions have the lowest tax impact, it would be best to utilize the fund within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on Calamos Convertible & High Income Fund to a Buy at this time. The CHY fund now trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, and the first set of interest rate cuts are likely to set a more positive momentum going forward. A lower interest rate would reduce the fund's risk related to the high use of leverage and improve the fund's operating spread. Additionally, the fund has a long track record of being able to preserve investor capital while keeping payouts consistent.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: 5 Business 'Elephants' It Can Afford To Buy
data.description:
There remain only a handful of companies in this country capable of truly moving the needle at Berkshire, and they have been endlessly picked over by us and by others. - Warren Buffett.

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.A, NYSE:BRK.B) has a cash problem: specifically, it has accumulated an enormous amount and has yet to find a sizeable acquisition to put it to work at higher rates of return. As interest rates go down the urgency increases, though it has been years that Warren Buffett has been looking for a truly sizeable acquisition. As recently as 2019, he reaffirmed his desire, despite sky-high valuations for quality businesses, that they "...continue, nevertheless, to hope for an elephant-sized acquisition."

To a certain degree, it is not surprising that Buffett has been willing to let cash accumulate, as he thinks of it in a different way than most people. Buffett considers cash as a call option with no expiration date, on every asset class, and with no strike price. He has compared investing to baseball, saying you can simply "...sit there and watch pitch after pitch go by and wait for the one right in your sweet spot." At the same time, at some point you have to swing at an opportunity, or as Charlie Munger used to say "When one of these infrequent opportunities pops up, don't get caught sucking your thumb."

One interesting effect of accumulating such an unbelievable hoard of cash and short-term investments is that they are now in a league of their own in terms of being able to make acquisition offers for companies that are too big for most investors, perhaps with the exception of a handful of sovereign wealth funds. Still, Berkshire Hathaway also has an advantage over sovereign wealth funds as an acquisition by them of a well-known company would likely be less controversial. In any case, Berkshire ended last quarter with $344 billion in cash and short-term investments, and we know Buffett would like at least a few tens of billions to remain on the balance sheet. It is worth noting that not only is cash and short-term investments close to a record in absolute terms, but as a percentage of total assets it is almost twice the ten-year average, and almost three times the 10% low it reached in 2016.

This means Berkshire Hathaway is in a great position to make an acquisition offer. This is especially true at a time when leveraged buyouts (LBOs) have to consider elevated financing costs, and many are having difficulty selling their existing investments.

With that in mind we decided to search for potential elephant-sized acquisitions Berkshire could pursue. A premium would likely have to be offered to convince existing shareholders to sell, which is why we believe that Buffett's elusive business elephant would likely have a current market cap between $100 billion and $240 billion. Using some filters to remove companies that have not grown revenues over the past five years, operate in commodity industries, have low returns on equity (ROE), Price/Earnings above 20x, or not based in developed markets, we are left with less than a dozen companies.

From this already short list, there are five companies we think could be very attractive to Buffett, based on his investing philosophy. We'll make the case for each of the five, though all of them would face significant hurdles to be completed, even if shareholders want to sell.

Chubb

Perhaps the most obvious one from the list is Chubb Limited (CB), as this Zurich-based insurer turned out to be the mystery stock that Berkshire Hathaway kept confidential during several quarters, as it bought nearly 26 million shares, worth roughly $6.7 billion when it was revealed. Interestingly Berkshire has also exited positions in Markel (MKL) and Globe Life (GL). Some analysts have argued that Chubb's business fits well with Berkshire's existing insurance operations.

Chubb's business mix of primarily commercial lines property-casualty coverage and high-end homeowners' coverage dovetails well with Berkshire's existing mix of business.

The company's disciplined underwriting culture and global diversification mirror Buffett's own GEICO and Berkshire Hathaway Reinsurance operations. With $51 billion in net premiums written in 2024 and consistent growth across both Property and Casualty (P&C) and life insurance segments, Chubb offers exactly what Buffett seeks, predictable float generation and superior underwriting returns. What makes Chubb particularly attractive is its premium positioning in commercial insurance markets, serving high-net-worth individuals and sophisticated corporate clients, segments that command pricing power and exhibit lower loss ratios.

Chubb's strong balance sheet, global scale, and proven management team led by Evan Greenberg would add immediate value to Berkshire's insurance empire, while giving Buffett the ability to deploy capital into an industry he understands better than perhaps any other investor alive.

Disney

The Walt Disney Company (DIS) represents a rare opportunity to acquire one of the world's most valuable intellectual property portfolios at what may prove to be a generational discount. While the company has faced challenges with streaming profitability and linear television decline, its fundamental assets remain unparalleled. The Disney, Pixar, Marvel, Star Wars, and ESPN franchises are beloved by millions, and culturally the Disney brand is arguably as American as Coca-Cola (KO). This is why there would likely be massive push-back if a foreign buyer attempted a takeover, and Buffett might be one of the few buyers that management and politicians might approve.

The streaming business has finally turned profitable, parks and experiences continue to generate massive cash flows, and the company's pricing power in both filmed entertainment and theme parks demonstrates the kind of economic moat Buffett prizes.

Disney's challenges are largely execution-focused rather than structural, which plays to Berkshire's strength of providing patient capital and operational autonomy. While Disney's capital intensity and technological disruption risks would typically concern Buffett, the company's unmatched content library and globally recognized brands create barriers to entry that few entertainment companies can match. Disney offers Buffett a chance to buy one of America's most iconic companies while it's facing solvable challenges rather than insurmountable ones. Bob Iger returning as CEO was always meant to be temporary, selling to Buffett would be a graceful exit that "saves" Disney rather than admitting defeat.

Qualcomm

Qualcomm Incorporated (QCOM) represents an intriguing departure from Buffett's traditional comfort zone, but the semiconductor giant's business model increasingly resembles a toll-road operator, which Buffett has long appreciated. The company's patent portfolio and licensing agreements generate predictable, high-margin revenues from nearly every smartphone, and increasingly, from automotive and IoT devices. Qualcomm has annual revenues exceeding $38 billion, with operating margins consistently above 25%, demonstrating the kind of pricing power and profitability that catches Buffett's attention.

While Buffett famously avoided technology investments for decades, his Apple (AAPL) position demonstrates a willingness to embrace technology businesses with strong competitive positions and recurring revenue streams. Qualcomm's licensing model ensures that even competitors must pay to play, creating a business moat that could persist for decades as new patents are added and the technology improved.

The U.S. government blocked Broadcom's attempted acquisition of Qualcomm in 2018 despite Broadcom being domiciled in Singapore, citing concerns about weakening American leadership in 5G technology. While Berkshire doesn't pose similar foreign influence concerns, CFIUS and other national security agencies might question whether Berkshire's passive investment approach would maintain Qualcomm's R&D intensity.

Deutsche Telekom

Deutsche Telekom AG (OTCQX:DTEGF) might seem an unlikely target given Buffett's historical aversion to European companies and telecommunications, but the German giant's transformation into essentially a T-Mobile holding company creates a compelling investment case. Through its majority stake in T-Mobile (TMUS), Deutsche Telekom offers Buffett indirect ownership of America's most dynamic wireless carrier, a business generating strong free cash flow, consistent customer growth, and improving margins. The company has improved its capital structure while maintaining a dominant position in both Germany and the U.S. markets, with T-Mobile continuing to take market share from AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ) through aggressive pricing and superior network quality.

The strategic appeal for Berkshire lies in Deutsche Telekom's evolution into a stable, regulated utility-like business with growth characteristics. Telecommunications infrastructure in mature markets generates the kind of predictable cash flows Buffett has long favored in businesses like utilities and energy infrastructure.

For Berkshire, acquiring Deutsche Telekom would provide exposure to essential communications infrastructure across two of the world's largest economies, with the T-Mobile stake alone potentially justifying much of the purchase price.

Progressive

The Progressive Corporation (PGR) represents perhaps the most strategically obvious acquisition target for Berkshire Hathaway among these five candidates. As America's largest auto insurer by market share, Progressive has systematically outperformed competitors through superior use of data analytics, disciplined pricing, and operational excellence. The company's combined ratio consistently runs in the low-to-mid 90s, demonstrating underwriting profitability. Progressive's direct distribution model and Snapshot telematics program have created competitive advantages in customer acquisition and risk selection that would complement GEICO's existing capabilities, while providing Berkshire with dominant market share in a stable, mature industry.

Progressive's strength in bundled homeowners and auto insurance, commercial auto coverage, and usage-based insurance programs would fill gaps in GEICO's product lineup while providing cross-selling opportunities across Berkshire's customer base. The company's technology investments and data analytics capabilities rank among the best in the industry, and these capabilities could enhance underwriting across all of Berkshire's insurance operations. While acquiring a direct competitor to GEICO might seem counterintuitive, Berkshire has never been shy about consolidating industries.

The reason we think this is the least likely acquisition to happen is that such a merger would almost certainly trigger aggressive antitrust scrutiny. State insurance regulators would also need to approve the transaction, and likely requested remedies would probably involve either divesting GEICO or significant portions of Progressive's operations.

Takeaway

These five companies represent rare opportunities to deploy capital at scale in businesses with sustainable competitive advantages, strong management teams, and reasonable valuations. However, the regulatory and strategic hurdles range from manageable to potentially insurmountable. We see Chubb and Disney as the most feasible, while Deutsche Telekom offers enormous potential but would take Berkshire outside of its comfort zone. Qualcomm and Progressive would likely attract the most scrutiny, but there might be a way to structure the deals to overcome regulatory hurdles.

One thing that is clear is that just as he said in the 2023 shareholder letter, there are just a handful of companies that could truly move the needle for Berkshire. Ironically, the amount of cash and short-term investments means the company can actually make offers very few investors could match.

Berkshire and Buffett also have the advantage that they might receive less push-back if they attempt to acquire a company like Disney, compared to the reaction if a sovereign wealth fund made an offer. As we recently saw with the OxyChem deal with Occidental Petroleum (OXY), Berkshire is still willing to make acquisitions if the terms are attractive, and rival bidders were at a disadvantage given Berkshire's long-term relationship with the company. It will be interesting to see if Berkshire manages to complete the massive deal that so far has eluded Buffett. If it turns out to be Disney, it might actually reduce the regret of having sold in the sixties the stake that Berkshire then owned.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Canaan: I'm In With Call Options As Sentiment Has Finally Turned Around
data.description:
I initiate my coverage on Canaan Inc. (NASDAQ:CAN) with a strong buy rating after I noticed clear signs of a much-needed turnaround in the sentiment surrounding this stock.

To me, fundamentals alone are not enough to buy a stock. That's why I never considered adding this stock to my portfolio since the decline at the end of last year, despite the top-line growth (please notice that the decline I'm referring to is part of a broader decline since the ATHs in 2021). My investment style, which I mostly consider akin to gambling, needs a story, a compelling narrative, followed by irrational optimism. I see these signs in Canaan (and the broader crypto space), which made me start a highly speculative position in the form of call options.

First, on September 8, Luxor began offering financing to Canaan’s mining customers. More than 5,000 A15 Pro units were sold in August to a leading U.S. miner. Second, on September 30, the company announced a 20 MW deployment at Soluna in Texas using the A15 XP, slated to start in Q1 2026. The icing on the cake happened with the October 2 announcement, when Canaan announced the largest order in three years; over 50,000 A15 Pro units for a U.S. customer with delivery in Q4 2025. All these announcements come on top of high top-line growth, with the Street projecting double-digit growth for the next few quarters.

On a valuation basis, the stock looks quite attractive, trading below sales on a TTM basis at the time of writing this article. Overall, I believe there is plenty of upside ahead with tailwinds in the crypto space that could lead to more CapEx being deployed. Below, I lay out the pillars of my bull case.

A Turn Around In Sentiment

On September 8, the Canaan announced a partnership with Luxor Technology Corporation to expand financing for institutional buyers. According to the terms, Luxor will now offer financing directly to Canaan's mining customers for purchasing the Avalon miners. Unfortunately, I haven't been able to find any specific rates or tenors that could benefit from this partnership, aside from the following statement:

The partnership already facilitated the sale of more than 5,000 Avalon® A15 Pro bitcoin mining machines in August to a leading U.S.-based institutional bitcoin miner.

Interestingly enough, Canaan's stock was flat on September 8 and September 9.

However, as you can see above, September 8 was the spark of the bull run that happened in the following weeks. I believe the market was not that convinced that the partnership would change the sentiment, which is why the reaction around September 8 and 9 was muted.

On September 30, the company announced a 20 MW deployment with Soluna at a wind powered data center in Texas. According to the deal, Soluna selected the Avalon A15 XP Bitcoin miner for this deployment, with a planned start in Q1 2026. Even though no pricing, contract length, hosting rate, revenue split, or capex/opex allocation were disclosed, this news was enough for the market to regain the much-needed optimism in Canaan, and following the announcement, the stock climbed by 18%. This is when the stock popped up in my screener. However, on October 1, I was still not convinced. Why?

Price action. On October 1, I was looking at the chart below and I was trying to understand if the recent news were enough to turn around the sentiment and break out beyond $1.

Back then, I had a look at fundamentals, and I couldn't really understand why the stock was trading below $1, considering the top-line growth for the past four quarters.

From my experience as a market practitioner, fundamentals and (emphasis) price action have to make sense for a turnaround story to take place. Buying just on strong fundamentals, when the chart looks like the Niagara waterfalls (to put it nicely), is the number one rookie mistake that I see, especially around value investors reluctant to look at charts (stating the random walk argument, which I believe is absolutely wrong). That's why I was hesitant to buy on October 1. I am the type of investor who would happily pay a premium on the share price at the expense of higher conviction, especially when I am dealing with risky, overpriced options.

The announcement on October 2 was the final confirmation that I (and I believe many investors) needed to open a position. That day, Canaan secured its largest order in three years: a landmark US order, comprising over 50,000 Avalon A15 Pro miners from an undisclosed US customer, scheduled for Q4 2025 delivery. This was the perfect catalyst at the perfect time. As seen below, the share price gapped through the $1 resistance, jumping by 27%.

That was the second-highest day by trading volume for the stock, with 121 million shares exchanging hands.

To me, this last order is the ultimate evidence of a recovering US mining market. Back in August, Riot deployed hashrate up to 36.4 EH/s, with BTC production up by 48% yoy. CleanSpark recently added $100M bitcoin-backed capacity with Coinbase Prime and another $100M facility with Two Prime. From what I'm reading, these funding rounds were explicitly to fund energy and compute growth. With the Bitcoin price in a consolidation phase, trading between $100k - $125k, I believe there is plenty of upside ahead for the US-based miners once Bitcoin breaks out beyond $125k. Again, as an active market practitioner, I believe a breakout after a consolidation phase is a hyper bullish signal.

Valuation

On a valuation basis, I see a cheap company, trading below sales on a TTM basis. When looking at the projections for next year, the P/S multiple is 1, which is 70% below the sector median.

Given that the company is not profitable, we cannot talk about P/E multiples. All we have is top-line growth. As seen below, the figures that the Street is projecting are really attractive.

Cheap multiples, combined with strong top-line growth and an improvement in sentiment, are music to my ears. That said, there are risks to my bull case.

Risks

I strongly believe that the growth figures that the Street is projecting above have priced in a significant price increase in BTC. Considering the high volatility of Bitcoin, the biggest risk that I see for my thesis is a decline in BTC price, which would slow down the CapEx deployment of miners, as they would have less incentive to operate their centers at full steam. Naturally, this would depress the number of new orders for Canaan's mining machines, leading to a deterioration of sentiment and fundamentals.

My bet with Canaan is that this is not going to happen in the near term. With more rate cuts ahead (CME projects rates between 350-375 bps before year-end, with 88% certainty) and a softer dollar (dollar sits sub 98 on DXY), I believe Bitcoin has strong tailwinds to break from the consolidation phase that I mentioned before. Also, historically, October has been BTC’s best month, with a median 10.8% return.

Conclusion

I stick my neck out with my strong buy rating, and I bought January 16, $1 call options to back up my thesis. Overall, I believe the recent partnership with Luxor will lead to more deals with institutional US-based buyers. The October 2 announcement, being the largest order in three years, is a strong sign that the miners are deploying CapEx again. With tailwinds in the crypto space, mainly from further rate cuts, a softer dollar, and favorable seasonality in October, I believe we would see a breakout beyond $125k before year-end. What does this mean for Canaan?

Well, in this scenario, I believe US-based miners would have more incentives to operate, which will likely increase their CapEx needs, leading to more purchases from Canaan.

data.title: MasterBrand: Undervalued Play With Upside Potential Thanks To American Woodmark Deal
data.description:
Shareholders of MasterBrand, Inc. (NYSE:MBC) will most likely benefit quite a bit from the deal with American Woodmark Corporation (AMWD) because of AMWD's recent investments in a new ERP cloud solution and CRM as well as the total synergies expected from the deal. The location of the manufacturing facilities reported by both companies indicates that the new entity could merge many manufacturing and distribution facilities in Florida and the southern part of the United States. In addition, AMWD is most likely very interested in the deal because it will make a lot of new money thanks to the existing dealer network owned by MBC. If the deal does not work, we may be left with shares of MasterBrand, which appears substantially undervalued if we consider the company's free cash flow and current EV/FWD EBITDA ratio.

MasterBrand, American Woodmark, Manufacturing Locations, And The Deal

MasterBrand presents itself as the largest residential cabinet manufacturer in North America. The company provides clients with an diversified mix of cabinets and options. The company profits from a distribution network of over 7.7k dealers and retailers, and reports around 13k employees and 20 manufacturing facilities. Founded in 1954 and given the total number of employees, infrastructure, and deals with distributors, Masterbrand appears to be a proven business model. The following is a map I could find about the locations and distribution centers reported by MasterBrand.

American Woodmark is not as large as MBC. It reports 18 manufacturing and distribution facilities and 8 primary service centers. According to its sites, American Woodmark has more than 7k team members. I consulted a map with the location of the manufacturing facilities reported by American Woodmark.

Bain is the firm in charge of reviewing the potential synergies of the merger signed by MBC and American Woodmark. Bain calculated a total of $90 million in total run-rate cost synergies. From here, I have much less information than Bain. Having said that and considering the previous map offered, I think that the new entity will be able to merge many of the manufacturing and distribution facilities in Florida and the southern part of the United States.

The combined entity is expected to have total revenue of $4.5 billion, an adjusted EBITDA margin of 14%, and net debt to adjusted EBITDA of 2x. If we assume a pro forma equity value of $2.4 billion and an enterprise value of $3.6 billion, the company's EV/EBITDA would stand at 5.6x, which looks quite cheap. Seeking Alpha noted that the sector median in the sector is not far from 12x FWD EBITDA.

MasterBrand Looked For A Merger From January 2025

I studied carefully the background of the merger, where I found relevant information about the intention of each company in January and February 2025. I found out that both companies are looking to sign an agreement for several reasons. The following information was obtained from SEC filings. The investors only looking at the presentations given by management may not know the following details. MasterBrand was studying potential geographic expansion, potential synergies, and the results of a combination of products with other targets.

In February 2025, American Woodmark had conversations with its Board of Directors with respect to potential M&A activities. According to management, the company needed new dealer channels in new regions and expansion into a semi-customer product line. With a merger, American Woodmark would most likely accelerate these two business drivers. It is worth noting that MBC appears to have a large network of dealers that American Woodmark could profit from. As soon as the new dealers receive products from American Woodmark, I would expect the total net revenue to increase substantially. Besides, as soon as American Woodmark is able to sell the products from MBC to its existing customers on the East Coast, total revenue from the new business entity could multiply. Considering revenue from both entities and the revenue catalysts, I assumed that 2026 total revenue of $4.3-$4.4 billion is feasible.

MBC Could Benefit From The New ERP Cloud Solution And CRM that American Woodmark Was Preparing Right Before The Merger Agreement

The digital transformation efforts implemented by American Woodmark could also help MBC. According to the Accounting and Finance journal, the adoption of ERP systems is associated with sustained operational efficiencies and improved liquidity. Also, Robin Poston from Michigan State University also noted that the implementation of ERPs reduces costs by improving efficiencies through computerization and enhances decision-making.

American Woodmark has been making a lot of efforts to accelerate the digital transformation of the business. I think that this is a beneficial fact that I did not find in the merger presentation. The company is right now implementing a new ERP cloud solution and a CRM tool that could bring substantial new efficiencies. Thanks to these efforts, I expect EBITDA margin of around 14% from 2026.

Balance Sheet Review

Employees will most likely understand and be prepared for the merger because MBC reported a number of acquisitions in the past. In the last quarter, MBC reported cash worth $120 million, with total assets of $2.9 billion and goodwill worth $1.1 billion. The total amount of goodwill indicates that M&A is a relevant strategy for MBC.

The merger will be beneficial for MBC because it will reduce the company's net debt/EBITDA levels. With total long term debt of $998 million, total liabilities stand at $1.59 billion. In sum, MBC appears to have a significant amount of debt, so a deal with another company could enhance MBC's financing options.

Many debt investors would perceive that the ratio of net debt to adjusted EBITDA is large, and the company may have to pay a lot of money to receive additional debt. According to the most recent quarterly report, the net debt to adjusted EBITDA stands at 2.5x. With the new M&A agreement, I would be expecting a decline in MBC's total interest expenses, which could lead to increases in net income and free cash flow.

MasterBrand Appears To Be A Buy Without The Merger: A DCF Model And My Own Model

According to the expectations of management, MasterBrand could deliver unlevered free cash flow between $137 million and $324 million from 2025 to 2029 with 2029 EBITDA of $556 million. The DCF model offered by the financial adviser in the deal included a valuation of about $14.25 to $21.78. However, I believe that the company is worth more than the range observed by the financial advisor in the deal.

I think that the company's current facility footprint and recent increases in capacity will most likely lead to earnings growth, and FCF growth. The net property, and equipment increased from close to $400 million in 2024 to more than $540 million in 2025.

I used a constant free cash flow of $280 million, which is in line with the free cash flow reported by MBC in the last few years. According to Seeking Alpha, MasterBrand reported FCF of $354 million in 2023 and $248 million in 2024.

My numbers are a bit higher than the initial expectations given by management. In my view, the company may be cautious of the 50% tariff on cabinetry and related products, or changes in the price of raw materials. In this regard, it is worth mentioning that MBC usually delivers better than expected financial figures. From 2022 to 2025, MBC reported better than expected EPS almost always. In Q1 2023, the EPS surprise stood at close to 107%, and in Q2 2023, and Q3 2023 the EPS stood at 35%.

With net debt of $954 million, weighted average shares outstanding of 129.9 million, WACC of around 6%, cost of debt of 5%, cost of equity of 7%, debt weight of 37%, beta of 0.8, and long term growth of 2%, I obtained a price target of $49 per share.

There is substantial upside potential if we also take into account the current EV/EBITDA and the competitors in the same sector, which trade at about 12x FWD EBITDA. Considering the EV/EBITDA valuation of peers, I think that an upside potential of over 30% is likely.

Synergies And Valuation Of The New Entity, Which Also Looks Quite Cheap

If the companies merge, American Woodmark shareholders will receive 5.150 shares of MasterBrand common stock. Synergies are estimated at $90 million. They are expected to be achieved by the end of the third year following completion of the transaction. According to the merger agreement, the new entity is expected to have 2029 net sales of $5.2 billion, with an adjusted EBITDA of $926 million and pro forma free cash flow of $555 million. 2025 adjusted EBITDA would stand at $510 million with FCF of $172 million.

The implied 2025 EV/adjusted EBITDA of the new entity is substantially smaller as compared to the sector median. But that's not all. I really do not think that the companies together will deliver such small numbers. MBC reported unlevered 2023 FCF of $354 million and 2024 FCF of $248 million. Besides, AMWD reported annual FCF of about $83 million, $95 million, and $184 million in the past. 2025 FCF of only $172 million appears too small for the free cash flow generated by both companies.

Risks

Higher tariff costs applicable to raw materials could destroy future free cash flow. MasterBrand and American Woodmark Corporation report manufacturing facilities in the United States. However, both companies acquire raw materials and components from third parties in Asia. In the future, changes in tariff costs could erode the financial statements of MasterBrand and American Woodmark Corporation. The Trump Administration’s 50% tariff on cabinetry and related products may be great for both companies in the short term. However, if other countries respond to the increase in tariffs in the US, I would not be that happy about the future of the industry.

If MasterBrand cannot negotiate with debt holders, I would expect an increase in interest expenses and a decrease in the intrinsic valuation obtained in DCF models. The cost of debt and the cost of equity would increase. Masterbrand reports a substantial amount of debt. The company will have to pay $386 million in debt payments, interest payments, and other purchase obligations in 2029.

Conclusion

MasterBrand and American Woodmark looked for a transaction for different reasons, but will most likely benefit from the deal they recently signed. In my view, the location of their manufacturing facilities and distribution centers will bring a number of cost synergies that could enhance the financial performance of the future entity. American Woodmark would also benefit from the network of dealers owned by MasterBrand. In addition, MasterBrand will most likely benefit from the new ERP cloud solution and CRM that American Woodmark was developing right before the Agreement was signed. In sum, I think that under all case scenarios, MasterBrand looks like a buy. If the merger closes, shareholders will enjoy synergies of about $90 million and a large undervalued entity. If the merger does not close, we would own MasterBrand, which is undervalued given its free cash flow numbers and current EV/FWD EBITDA ratio.

data.title: Lithium Americas: Strategic For The U.S., Risky For Investors
data.description:
From the first look Lithium Americas Corp. (NYSE:LAC) like a very good story for the investor. Government getting involved, strategic US project, General Motors partnership, and Thacker Pass is called critical source for electro mobiles future. The company is in media is said to be national security question, and in my opinion, the investors fail to ask if this is really good investment. I believe currently no. Today LAC euphoria is more of a political play, than financial opportunity. In this article, I will give you my variant perception - many believe that LAC is a strategic project, but I see difficult capital structure, financing layers and so on. This thesis is not against the project itself, it's a thesis against idea that what company is currently doing benefits the shareholders.

Capital structure is layered against shareholders

The biggest problem that every “positive” news like in recent days, in reality, it pushes the shareholder to the back of the line. General Motors has 100% off take right to the first Thacker Pass phase 20 years ahead. DOE gave a loan with additional equity layer and warrants. US government got warrants of 5% in the company and 5% share in the project. So, what does it mean? That in line with the real value, shareholders are standing last. Partners, creditors and government will get their own, and shareholders will be left with what is left. This is classical example when “strategic project” sounds good in press, but in reality, capital structure is becoming more and more complicated and more against the retail investor.

In the simple mining company, the structure is: creditors then shareholders. But in LAC: GM offtake, DOE loan with warrants, government involvement and partnerships. All this means that shareholders are getting their value, when all of the others are satisfied.

Increasing short interest

One of the strongest signals that something is wrong is shorts dynamics. In two weeks, short interest went up. This in my opinion is not a coincidence. I believe that shows that institutions are actively betting against this story. Market professionals already see disbalance between hype and real finance. And in this kind of environment, any bad news like CAPEX increase, late constructions, lithium price stagnation – could be a catalyst for a market correction. Take Interactive Brokers for example, it had collapsed lendable shares nearly to zero, signaling a surge in demand. This kind of tightening in shortable supply, often shows aggressive institutional positioning.

Lithium market

At this time lithium prices are around $9-10k a ton, this is few times lower than in 2022. Project which requires high CAPEX is sensitive to price cycles. If Thacker Pass starts at the same time, when the market is weakening, projects return could be minimal or even negative. No one can guarantee that lithium prices could get back to 2022 peak. And most of the NPV models today are based on optimistic scenarios, which not necessarily going to play out. In other words, the project could be a little disappointment when it starts.

Political plays are not bull cases

Mass media is presenting that LAC is a strategic project for US, and I do not doubt that. But let’s not forget, that government is involved not to create shareholder value, but to secure critical minerals supply. This means that the decisions are going to be made based on political, not economic motives. If needed they can make additional regulations, increase costs, or restrict freedom to distribute profits. I hope this is not the case, but if this happens, shareholders will become spectators, not beneficiaries.

Lithium Americas today is a story, where hype around “strategic US project” overruns financial realities, and we will talk about financials below. The capital structure is increasingly layered in favor of partners and the state, while ordinary shareholders are left in the back. Short interest is already signaling that some market players already see this problem.

Good things about the company

Even though I am bearish, I can't ignore that Lithium Americas has some strong sides and potential. Thacker Pass is one of the biggest lithium projects in US, which has more that 40 million tons of LCE, and this gives a real scale advantage. US government attention to local supply chain is very favorable - federal loans, GM partnership and government investment shows that his project will be pushed even in bad economic conditions. In other words, this is not a simple miner, but a project which Washington sees as part of national security.

The EV demand is not going anywhere, and I have doubts it will. If lithium prices could stabilize around $15-20k per ton, projects ROI would look attractive, and NPV would be higher than current capitalization (see below). This perspective creates bull case, which attracts institutional investors and retail as well.

However, the current valuation is giving already optimistic scenario, investors have already priced in Thacker Pass project, EV market demand, and better lithium prices. So, the current price is not a cheap lottery ticket to the future, but it's a bet, that the best scenario plays out. And if one of the elements (costs, terms, prices or politics) disappoint we could see a sharp price correction.

Financial Health and Results

If we look into balance and results, it becomes clear that the company in this phase does not have stable financial health. It is not able to generate profits, and investments are primarily funded through debt, government and partner injections.

Today, LAC is still pre-revenue. Thacker Pass is not commercially available, due to this reason, the company still does not have any revenue. In last 12 quarters they couldn’t show any sustainable profitability. For example, in Q2 2025 the loss was around -$28 million. This means that LAC is surviving from investors and partners, not real operations.

In 2025 June, LAC had $509 million cash and equivalents. It looks decent, but the problem is that the cash flows from operations are always negative, and investment flows are very high. Construction works and engineering contracts with Bachtel require hundreds of millions, so this buffer is running out fast. If they somehow fail to attract capital (which is currently not the case), the money will dry up before getting first revenue.

Thacker Pass first phase construction costs alone are at around $2.9 billion, this is higher than their market capitalization, and it’s clear that project risk is bigger than financial spine. If project experiences problems, they would be saving government and partners interests (DOE debt, GM offtake, US government share), and shareholders would be the last one to be saved.

I believe results are not getting better they are just being delayed. In my opinion, all communications about progress – signed EPCM contract, construction start, DOE loan – is not real financial results. Just new obligations, which are increasing risk. The main problem – non-existent cashflow generation is still present. The company increasing their dependency from external sources and I don’t see evidence that the situation would change in short term.

All projects financial models are basing that lithium price will stay in the upper lever. But this is far from reality as well, for the second year the price is down. In 2025 lithium carbonate price is hovering around $10-12k per ton LCE, when in 2022 peak, it was $70k. If this situation persists, projects IRR will be quite lower than expected, and that means financial situation could become critical.

In last feasibility study, Thacker Pass first phase OPEX is around $5-6k/t LCE. This is quite low metric, if we compare it to the worldwide average ($7-8k/t), though it still leaves project sensitive to price cycles.

If we look at liquidity, in 2025 they had $509 million in cash, and quarterly burn rate is around $25-30 million in operating expenses + hundreds of millions in CAPEX. If the current construction burns cash at the same rate, the company might have to issue new shares to finance it.

All in all, LAC results are not getting better – in my opinion they are getting camouflaged in political and strategic news. Cash is running out, CAPEX is as high as it can be and revenue does not exist.

Valuation

I have done a DCF analysis based on Thacker Pass 20-year lifespan. In my DCF I have put CAPEX ($2.9 billion), yearly manufacturing (40k tons LCE), OPEX ($5k/t) and WACC (10%). Calculated yearly EBITDA in each scenario (Bear $10k/t, base $15k/t, bull $25k/t). Current NPV divided by 160 million shares, shows fair value as you can see in the table, which in bearish scenario was negative. Base case is around zero, and bull was the only positive above current market capitalization. Today's stock is pricing in the optimistic scenario, but not the current one.

Scenario Lithium Price (USD/t) Annual EBITDA NPV NPV vs Market Cap Fair Value/Share
Bear 10,000 200,000,000 -1,197,287,000 -1,897,287,000 -7.48
Base 15,000 400,000,000 505,425,500 -194,574,500 3.16
Bull 25,000 800,000,000 3,910,851,000 3,210,851,000 24.44

However, doing simple DCF is not good enough for company like this. I have done sensitivity analysis as well. Results show that, projects value is very fragile: if OPEX rises to $7k/t the base scenario you see above, becomes negative. Almost the same happens if WACC rises to 12% (which could happen if macro situation worsens), NPV would be $0.55 per share.

Another important factor I tried to simulate was capital attraction. With $2.9 billion CAPEX need and $509 million current cash, the new share issuance is very likely. If company manages to get $1-1.5 billion dollars with current market conditions, let's say issuing new shares $5 each, the outstanding shares would double. Then base NPV value per share would fall from $3.16 to $1.1-1.4.

This means that even bull scenario is heavily weakened, when we include real capital needs and sensitivity to costs or interest rates.

Summary

Lithium Americas (LAC) currently is looking more political than financial project. Thacker Pass is strategically important for US - it is supported by General Motors, DOE and government, however multi layered capital structure leaves shareholders in the back, which is not ideal. GM has offtake rights, DOE - loans with warrants, and government involvement. At the same time, the company is still pre revenue: losses are continuing, and $509 million cash is small reserve against $2.9 billion CAPEX need. I believe that the shareholders could get diluted when the capital runs out.

DCF analysis shows that at current lithium prices ($9-12k/t), project does not create value, and base scenario ($15k/t) is balancing near zero if we make more than base calculations. Only optimistic scenario is giving actually big upside. That is why today it is priced in, and investors are facing more risk than reward. I will be rating this stock a Strong Sell. Not a shorting position just yet, but not holding one either.
